steps.title: Identify the sampling population
steps.content:
The 200 people were selected at random from people who indicated they liked the book.

In survey research, you can only appropriately generalize from a sample to the population from which it was drawn.

Our sampling population is therefore people who like the book, and the sample result was 95% disliked the movie.

steps.title: Eliminate invalid inferences
steps.content:
Check each choice against the sampling principle:
  • A: Generalizes to all people who go see movies — we didn't sample from them
  • B: Generalizes to all people who read books — we sampled only those who liked THIS book
  • C: Makes a claim about people who dislike the book — our sample contained zero people from this group; we have no data on them
  • D: Makes a claim about people who like the book — this is exactly our sampling population

steps.title: Verify the appropriate inference
steps.content:
Our sample of people who like the book showed that 95% disliked the movie.

Choice D claims most people who like this book will dislike this movie.

Since most means more than 50%, and our sample shows 95%, this inference is directly supported by the data about the correct population.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
